---
title: "Research"
---

## Language acquisition and grammar induction

My current research asks how deep networks can learn hierarchical linguistic structure from examples alone.

I study this question using probabilistic context-free grammars, synthetic languages, inside--outside algorithms, and neural architectures trained on structured data. A central goal is to understand which statistics make parsing learnable, how much data is required, and how these mechanisms relate to language acquisition in large neural networks.

My ICML 2026 paper, *Deep networks learn to parse uniform-depth context-free languages from local statistics*, studies a controlled family of context-free languages with varying tree topology and ambiguity. The work shows that deep networks can exploit local statistical regularities to construct parse trees, and connects their sample complexity to theoretically predicted correlation strengths.

## Statistical physics of learning

A central theme in my work is the use of statistical-physics ideas to understand learning: phase transitions, sample complexity, scaling laws, and the emergence of latent structure from data.

This perspective is particularly useful for language-like data, where the observable sequence is generated by hidden hierarchical structure. I am interested in how simple models can reveal the mechanisms by which neural networks discover such structure.

## Previous work in statistical physics

Before moving into AI and language learning, I worked on the statistical physics of amorphous solids, with a focus on aging, yielding, avalanches, and driven disordered systems.

My Ph.D. developed mean-field theories for the aging and yielding of athermal amorphous solids. This work led to first-author publications in *Physical Review Letters*, *Physical Review Materials*, and *Physics of Fluids*. During my subsequent postdoctoral work in Göttingen, I developed mean-field approaches to ductile and brittle yielding transitions beyond standard random-field-Ising-model paradigms.

This background continues to shape my approach to machine learning: I am especially interested in simple models, emergent structure, phase transitions, and the relation between microscopic rules and macroscopic behavior.

## Methods and tools

My work combines:

- probabilistic modeling
- statistical mechanics
- grammar induction and parsing
- inside and inside--outside algorithms
- deep learning and transformer models
- synthetic data generation
- scalable PyTorch implementations
- numerical experiments on GPU/HPC systems
